name: How to Document Workplace Toxins When You Suspect Your Job is Making You Sick

step.name: Step 1
step.text:
How to Document Workplace Toxins When You Suspect Your Job is Making You Sick

Suspecting that your workplace is the cause of your health problems is a heavy realization. Whether it is a strange rash, a persistent cough, or unexplained fatigue, you need to move from 'suspecting' to 'documenting.' In Utah and Idaho, the strength of your workers' compensation claim for an occupational illness depends entirely on the evidence you collect. Here is a step-by-step guide on how to protect yourself legally while you seek medical help.

Step 1: Identify the Potential Culprits

Look around your workspace. Are there strong chemical smells? Is there visible dust or mold? Are you working in an area with poor ventilation? Make a list of every substance you handle or are near. This includes cleaning agents, industrial solvents, metal dust, or even office supplies in a poorly ventilated space. If your employer provides 'Safety Data Sheets' (SDS), ask for them. Employers are legally required to keep these for every chemical on-site.

Step 2: Start a Detailed Health and Work Journal

Correlation is often the first step to proving causation. Keep a daily log answering these questions:

  • What time did I start work and what was my first symptom?
  • Did my symptoms improve over the weekend or while I was on vacation?
  • Did a specific task (like mixing a certain chemical) trigger a flare-up?
This log creates a 'temporal' link that doctors can use to justify a work-related diagnosis.

Step 3: Take Photos and Videos

Visual evidence of your working conditions is powerful. If you see dust accumulating on surfaces, overflowing trash cans, or broken ventilation fans, take a picture. If you are not provided with proper Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) like respirators or gloves, or if the provided gear is worn out, photograph it. *Note: Be mindful of company policies regarding cell phone use in secure areas; if you cannot take photos, write a detailed description.*

Step 4: Talk to Your Coworkers

Occupational illnesses rarely happen in a vacuum. Ask your colleagues (discreetly) if they are experiencing similar symptoms. If three people in the same HVAC-controlled area are all having headaches and dizziness, it strongly suggests an environmental cause. You don't need to start a movement, but knowing who else is affected can provide a list of potential witnesses for your claim.

Step 5: See a Doctor and Be Specific

When you see a doctor, don't just say 'I feel sick.' Say, 'I think I am being exposed to [Substance] at work, and my symptoms began [Timeline].' Ensure the doctor notes your concerns in your official medical record. This 'initial report' is one of the most important documents in a workers' comp case.

Step 6: Consult with Just Work Comp Law

Once you have gathered this preliminary evidence, call a lawyer. We can help you formally request maintenance logs and air quality tests that an employer might otherwise withhold. Occupational illness claims have strict deadlines in Utah and Idaho. Contact us at 801-746-0290 to discuss your findings and start your claim correctly.
